title: : Please prepare a 600-800-word reflection on your experience participating in the

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
: Please prepare a 600-800-word reflection on your experience participating in the
online poverty simulation and synchronous class session/discussion (Community Advocates’
Panel and group breakout discussions). You may also wish to incorporate information learned
while engaging with other elements of the online pre-module

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
.

As an AI language model, I do not have personal experiences or thoughts but I can give you a summary reflection of what human authors might present in their reflection after participating in an online poverty simulation.

The online poverty simulation allowed me to experience, to a certain extent, the challenges and difficulties that individuals living in poverty face daily. The simulation was eye-opening, as it highlighted how difficult it can be to access basic necessities, such as food, housing, and healthcare when living in poverty. It was unsettling and alarming to witness the many obstacles that impoverished individuals face when attempting to access these necessities.

One of the most significant challenges I witnessed during the simulation was the limited resources available to individuals living in poverty. The simulation demonstrated that resources are scarce, and many individuals are forced to make difficult choices about where to allocate their limited resources. For instance, participants had to trade off between buying food or paying for rent, or deciding between receiving medical care or going to work, which could result in getting fired and being unable to pay rent or buy food.

Additionally, participating in the Community Advocates’ Panel and group breakout discussions provided me with a better understanding of the community resources available to individuals living in poverty. Discussing these resources emphasized the importance of community organizations and advocates, such as food banks, homeless shelters, and community clinics, in addressing poverty-related issues.

Moreover, the discussions highlighted various other issues related to poverty, such as racial and gender disparities. The Community Advocates’ Panel speakers discussed the impact of poverty on marginalized communities, such as African Americans, Indigenous people, and members of the LGBTQ+ community. The speakers shed light on how systemic racism and discriminatory policies affect people's access to resources and opportunities, exacerbating poverty in these communities.

Overall, the online poverty simulation and synchronous class session/discussion provided me with valuable insights into the complexity of poverty and how it affects individuals, families, and communities. It underscored the importance of social justice and the need for policymakers and community advocates to address poverty as a multifaceted issue by implementing policies and programs that take into account the different factors that impact poverty.

In conclusion, the online poverty simulation and synchronous class session/discussion spurred me to take a more serious look at poverty-related issues and to seek out ways that I can help support individuals and communities experiencing poverty. They helped me understand that poverty is not just an individual problem but requires a holistic approach by various stakeholders to facilitate change.

The primary efficacy end point was assessed
in the intention-to-treat population, which in-
cluded all eligible patients who underwent ran-
domization and had both week 4 and week 7
postsurgical blood specimens. A sensitivity analy-
sis was performed in the per-protocol popula-
tion, which included patients who had under-
gone 24-month surveillance imaging (unless
recurrence or death had already occurred) and,
for ctDNA-guided management, ctDNA-positive
patients who received at least 12 weeks of che-
motherapy or ctDNA-negative patients who re-
ceived no more than 4 weeks of chemotherapy.
Noninferiority of ctDNA-guided management to
standard management was to be accepted if the
lower bound of the 95% confidence interval
around the estimated difference in the 2-year
recurrence-free survival was above −8.5 percent-
age points. In addition, recurrence-free survival
and percentages of patients with recurrence
within 1, 2, and 3 years in a time-to-event
analysis were computed from the Kaplan–Meier
survival curves along with the associated 95%
confidence intervals. Hazard ratios and associ-
ated 95% confidence intervals were also report-
ed after evaluation of the proportional hazards
assumption with the use of the Schoenfeld re-
siduals test. The between-group difference in the
use of adjuvant chemotherapy was assessed as
percentages of patients in each group and as rela-
tive risk. No prespecified plan to control for
multiplicity of testing was made, and therefore
the 95% confidence intervals cannot be used to
infer effects. This analysis was conducted when
the last patient reached a minimum follow-up of 2 years. Statistical analyses were performed with
R software, version 3.6.1 (R Project for Statisti-
cal Computing), and SAS software, version 9.4
(SAS Institute).

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The authors of the study did not mention any multiple comparisons correction to control alpha, and they did not make any prespecified plan to control for the multiplicity of testing. Therefore, the 95% confidence intervals cannot be used to infer effects, so there may be an increased risk of false-positive results due to multiple comparisons.

The lack of multiple comparisons correction may have implications for the findings of the study. Without controlling for multiplicity, there is a higher risk of identifying false-positive results, which could lead to overestimation of the efficacy of ctDNA-guided management. The lack of correction for multiple comparisons can also increase the risk of type I errors, leading to the potential rejection of true null hypotheses.

Overall, while the study's sample size and design were carefully planned to ensure adequate power for statistical analysis, the lack of multiple comparisons correction raises concerns about the potential for false-positive results. As such, future studies may need to include multiple comparisons correction to reduce the risk of false positives and ensure the validity of the results.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: What is the purpose of multiplicity testing/multiple comparison corrections

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The purpose of multiplicity testing/multiple comparison corrections is to control the overall type I error rate or the probability of falsely rejecting a null hypothesis when it is actually true. Essentially, these corrections are needed to ensure that the probability of making even one type I error remains at an acceptable level, even when multiple hypotheses or tests are being conducted.

When multiple tests are conducted, the risk of a false-positive result increases, as each test adds to the number of statistical comparisons being made. This can lead to overestimation of significance and erroneous conclusions in research.

To address this issue, multiple comparison corrections can be implemented to adjust the threshold for statistical significance or control the false discovery rate. Multiple comparison corrections aim to reduce the number of false positives by using more stringent thresholds to evaluate statistical significance.

Examples of multiple comparison corrections include Bonferroni correction, Holm-Bonferroni correction, Sidak correction, and False Discovery Rate (FDR) correction.

turns.text:
what does this mean No prespecified plan to control for
multiplicity of testing was made, and therefore
the 95% confidence intervals cannot be used to
infer effects.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
This means that the study did not make a predetermined plan to control for multiple comparisons when analyzing the data. As a result, the estimated effects and 95% confidence intervals cannot be used to draw definitive conclusions because the probability of obtaining a false positive result due to multiple testing is increased.

The absence of a predetermined plan to control for multiplicity of testing implies that the study may have a higher risk of reporting false-positive results. This can happen when multiple statistical tests are calculated, particularly if the same data are used to test multiple hypotheses. In such a scenario, the more tests conducted, the more likely it is that a truly null hypothesis will be incorrectly rejected due to chance.

Therefore, in the absence of controlling for multiplicity, the interpretation of the findings from this study should be cautious as it may lead to incorrect conclusions. It is generally recommended that researchers should pre-specify multi-comparison corrections to minimize the likelihood of false positives.
